summary: low-level versus high-level refers to whether we program using instructions and data objects at the level of the machine . general versus targeted to an application domain refers . whether primitive operations of the programming language are widely applicable or fine-tuned to a domain . it is often easier to debug programs written in languages designed to be interpreted . the interpreter can produce error messages that are easy to relate to the source code . Python is a general-purpose programming language you can use effectively to buil problems . Python is not optimal for programs that have high reliability constraints . it is a relatively simple language that is easy to learn . Python is designed to be interpreted, it can provide the kind of runtime feedback that is especially helpful to novice programmers . the language is presented in dribs and drabs, as needed for this ulterior purpose . excellent online resources describe every aspect of the language . since its introduction by Guido von Rossum in 1990, it has undergone many changes . this version of Python cleaned up many of the inconsistencies in the design of Python 2. This means that most programs and libraries written for earlier versions of Python cannot be run using implementations of Python 3. 2.1 Installing Python and Python IDEs Today, most programmers prefer to use a text editor that is part of an integrated development environment (IDE). the code appearing in this book was created and tested using Anaconda . all of the Python IDEs provide syntax highlighting, auto completion, smart indentation, and an integrated debugger . a window containing a collection of tools will appear . Spyder is an IPython console running an interactive Python shell . you can type and execute Python commands in this window . the pane on the left is an edit window into which you type programs that can be saved and run . a command, often called a statement, instructs the interpreter to do something . the function 12 print outputs the string Yankees rule! to the window associated with the shell . two values were passed to print in the third statement . Python has four types of scalar objects: int is used to represent integers . float always include a decimal point (e.g., 3.0 or 3.17 or -28.72). literals of type float can be written using scientific notation . al 1.6E3 stands for 1.6*103, i.e., it is the same as 1600.0 . bool is used to represent the Boolean values True and False . none is a type with a single value . in a Spyder console, something that looks like In [1]: is a shell prompt indicating that the interpreter is expecting the user to type some Python code into the shell . the line below the prompt is produced when a interpreter evaluates the Python code entered at the prompt . the order of evaluation can be changed by using parentheses to group subexpressions, e.g., (x+y)*2 first adds x and y, and then multiplies the result by 2. Figure 2-3 Operators on types int and float The primitive operators on type bool are and, or, and not: a and b is True if at least one of a or b are True, and False otherwise . in Python, a variable is just a name, nothing more . an assignment statement associates the name to the left of the = symbol with the object denoted by the expression to the right of the . = symbol . despite what Juliet said, 15 names matter . variable names can contain uppercase and lowercase letters, digits . if executed, they will do the same thing to a human reader . when executed, the code fragments are quite different . it is therefore important to write programs so that they are easy to read . Python variable names are case-sensitive e.g., Romeo and romeo are different names . the reserved words in Python 3.8 are and as break class elif else for from in is not or True try assert continue except global lambda pass while async def await del False if nonlocal raise with finally import None return yiel Another good way to enhance the readability is to add comments . the statement x, y = 2, 3 binds x to 2 and y to 3 . all of the expressions on the right-hand side of the assignment are evaluated before any bindings are changed . this is convenient since it allows you to use multiple assignment to swap bindings of two variables . conditional statement describes the type of code that could occur at that point in a program . if the test evaluates to False, execution resumes at the code following the statement . for example, if x%2 == 0: print('Even') else: print ('Odd') otherwise: print . the value of the variable x is even . indentation is semantically meaningful in Python . for example, if the last statement in the above code were indented, it would be part of the block associated with the else, rather than the block of code following the conditional statement . a line of code that is too long to read easily can be broken into multiple lines on the screen . g each line on the screen, other than the last one, with a backslash () many Python programmers prefer using implied line continuations . x = 1111111111111115111111 110111111 11111111 111111 and 222222333222222 . the following code contains nested conditionals in both branches of the top-level if statement . if x%2 == 0: print('Divisible by 2 and not by 3') is The elif in the above code stands for "else if" it often convenient to use a compound Boolean expression in the test of a conditional . a simple solution would involve a sequence of eight if statements, each with a single print statement . if x%2 == 0 and y%2 != 0- and z%2: print(max(x, y, z)) . the variables are repeatedly tested for oddness . lue to a variable (answer), updating it when appropriate, and then printing the final value of the variable . it tests whether each variable is odd exactly once, and contains only a single print statement . conditional expressions are of the form expr1 if condition else expr2 . the class of branching programs is still quite limited . assume that each line of code takes one unit of time to execute . the running time does not grow with the size of the input to the program . a straight-line program has n lines of code . the study of the intrinsic difficulty of problems is the topic of computational complexity . we need only one more programming language construct, iteration, to allow us write programs of arbitrary complexity. we get to that in Section 2.5. 2.4 Strings and Input Objects of type str can be written using either single or double quotes . operator + means addition when applied to two numbers and concatenation . operator * is also over loaded. it means what you expect it to mean when its operands are both numbers . the expression n*s, where n is an int and s is a str, evaluates to an str with n repeats of s . the code 'a'*'a produces the error message TypeError: can't multiply sequence by non-int of type ‘str' the type checking in Python is not as strong as in some other programming languages (e.g., Java) but it is better in Python 3 than in Python 2 . the length of a string can be found using the len function . for example, the value of len('abc') is 3. In Python, all indexing is zero-based . the last element of the string of length 3 is accessed using the index 2 . pressions such as 'abc' have the value you might expect . if the value before the colon is omitted, it defaults to the length of the string . it is also possible to supply a third argument to select a non-contiguous slice . the second print statement produces a more appropriate output by combining the 50 and the % into a single argument of type str . we use the name of a type to convert values to that type . when a float is converted to an int, the number is truncated (not rounded) formatted string literals contain both sequences of characters and expressions bracketed by curly braces . the code print(f'int(num*fraction) produces the same output as the previous, more verbose, print statement . atement print(f'num*fraction:,.0f is fraction*100% of num:,') prints 15,000,000 is 50.0% of 30,000,000 because the , modifier instructs Python to use commas as thousands separators . the space could be avoided by executing print('Are you really ' + name + '?' . each of which produces a single string and passes that string as the only argument to print . the code will always print class ‘str'> because input always returns an object of type str, even if the user entered something that looks like an integer . the Unicode standard contains more than 120,000 characters covering 129 modern and historic scripts and multiple symbol sets . you can tell Python which encoding to use by inserting a comment of the form # -*- coding: encode name-* as the first or second line of your program . utf-8 instructs Python to use UTF-8, the most frequently used character encoding for webpages . you can directly enter code like print('Mluv anglicky?') which will print . if you have a special keyboard, they are all rather cumbersome . ething like num_x = int(input('How many times should I print the letter X?') to_print = '' # concatenate X to to_do . if the test evaluates to True, the program executes the loop body once, and then goes back to reevaluate the test. Figure 2-6 Flowchart for iteration We can write the kind of loop depicted in while statement . Figure 2-7 Squaring an integer, the hard way The code starts by binding the variable x to the integer 3 . it proceeds to square x by using repetitive addition . x == 0, x > 0, and x  0. The initial value of num_iterations will also be 0, and the loop body will never be executed . the loop test evaluates to False, and control proceeds to the code following the while statement . the loop terminates, but it prints a negative value . if the assignment statement inside the loop is also changed, to ans = ans + abs(x), the code works properly . executing a break statement terminates the loop in which it is contained and transfers control to the code immediately following the loop . k will terminate the inner loop . write a program that asks the user to input 10 integers and then prints the largest odd number that was entered . if no odd number was entered, it should print a message to that effect . code total = 0 for num in (77, 11, 3): total = total + num print(total) will print 91 . tuple is a sequence of values bound to variable . the range function takes three integer arguments: start, stop, and step . range(0, 3) and range(3) both produce the sequence 0, 1, 2. The numbers in the progression are generated on an “as needed” basis, so even expressions such as range(1000000) consume little memory . the number of iterations is not controlled by an explicit test . the answer is 0, 0, 1, 0, because the arguments to the range function in the line with for are evaluated just before thursday . the first value in the sequence it yields is assigned to the index variable, i . when the sequence is exhausted, the loop terminates . we already saw that the range(x) controlling the outer loop is evaluated the first time it is reached and not reevaluated for each iteration, so there are four iterations of the inner loop . x = 4 means that the inner for loop is reached four times . for statement can be used in conjunction with the in operator to conveniently iterate over characters of a string . total = 0 for c in '12345678': total = total + int(c) print(total) sums the digits in the string denoted by the literal . style matters when writing in English, but it's not an asset for a programmer . good programmers follow coding conventions designed to make programs easy to understand, rather than fun to read . some of its prescriptions are arbitrary, for example, using four spaces for indents . if everyone uses the same number of spaces, it is easier to read (and perhaps combine) code written by different people . the most important conventions have to do with naming . some programmers prefer to use what is often called camelCase . the most important is to avoid using a lowercase L or uppercase I or an uppercase O (which are easily confused with the number one) that's enough about conventions for now . we take a short break from learning Python, and use what you have already learned to solve some simple problems . the name Python was chosen as a tribute to the British comedy troupe Monty Python . this leads one to think that the name IDLE is a pun on Eric Idle . click on the wrench in the toolbar to open the Preferences window . the actual value of  is not 3, but it can have consequences that are not always desirable . it is possible to hand-simulate a program using pen and paper, or even a text editor . in 2016, over 85% of the pages on the web were encoded using UTF-8 .
